A related work I’d recommend is “The Way we Think” by Fauconnier & Turner, which tries to get a bit more constructive about metaphorical thinking. I always thought there must be a programming language paradigm latent in this theory but was never able to tease it out.

Hi, I'm Jack Armitage. I am a PhD student in Media & Arts Technologies in the Augmented Instruments Lab, Centre for Digital Music, Queen Mary University of London.

I have been studying music technology and musical instrument design for around 10 years. My undergrad was BSc Music, Multimedia & Electronics at the University of Leeds, and after that I was a research engineer at <http://www.roli.com|www.roli.com> and later <http://www.fxpansion.com|www.fxpansion.com> working on instrument quality and developing visuo-tactile materials and interfaces. My PhD focuses on craft in digital musical instrument design, and how one's "ability to craft" is impacted by the tools, methods and technologies used (<https://scholar.google.co.uk/citations?user=APvoBhUAAAAJ&hl=en>).

I was somehow exposed to Bret Victor's Inventing on Principle around 2012 at the end of my undergrad, and from there I became quite obsessed with the history of 'The New Medium'. While working in the music technology industry I was seeing everything through Bret Victor and Alan Kay's eyes; 10's of expert designers and engineers spending 10's of thousands of pounds and 6 months to explore singular, basic ideas, and as a result not having any time or money - or even awareness - of possible 'bigger ideas' that could be explored.

I started my PhD wanting to solve these problems by creating new tools and programming languages that treat instrument designers as artists (see 'Artists and the Art of the Luthier' by Bill Buxton). However I made the mistake of interviewing violin makers in my first study, which has taken me down the rabbit hole of embodied craft practice, which I am now trying to reconcile with digital technology :slightly_smiling_face:. In my spare time I compose and produce music, and 'live code' music onstage using Haskell (<http://tidalcycles.org>). My latest music project just launched on Tuesday: <http://offer.band>

Based in London, always happy to talk over email or meet up for a coffee, and host visits to the Instruments Lab (<http://instrumentslab.org>). Thank you <@U5TCAFTD3> for inviting me to the recent London meetup and subsequently to this group. I am very intrigued to learn more about the people in this group!

text:
<https://programmingmadecomplicated.wordpress.com/2018/11/30/the-everyday-reality/>

<@UCQ4BBFQ9> You raise some interesting questions in the end. I’m tempted to start talking about (accidental) complexity and how over time this stateful mess of a running system explodes into a fractal multi-dimensional tree of stateful subsystems and that we haven’t found any other way yet to tame this complexity other than rewinding time and start all over again — hence the restarts; but I’m sure you already knew this and your list of question was more rhetorical and meant to inspire.

Love the final question in your post:
>How much effective control do we actually exercise over the universal simulators that we bought and consider our “own” posessions?

I don’t have an answer and this can be taken very different directions.

I like to think into the direction of how that complexity takes our superpowers away slowly, and how we somehow don’t feel the need to rectify that situation and instead continue piling more abstractions on top of the ones that we hardly understand anymore.

attachments.title: The everyday reality
attachments.text:
Why, after finally giving in and upgrading to MacOS Mojave, is the “restore previous session” button grayed out? I took great care to terminate Firefox explicitly and cleanly before I let the computer have its way and restart.
I had loads of tabs open. This is normal for me. Normally I use an explicit tab-saving extension on other browsers, but this lets me grow the number of simultaneous unfinished threads without bound.
So when I got a new MacBook, I took it as an opportunity to make a fresh start – no Chrome, no tab saver; just one restorable session that might ensure some browsing discipline.
Before long, of course, it wants to update. And, of course, update requires restart, because why not. And of course, restart means close all open applications and explicitly re-open them again later. But there’s a handy little button: “remind me tomorrow”. So my work stays undisrupted for a long time.
But then weird things start happening. The other day, I found that my MacBook was no longer charging when connected to mains power, and it was running low on battery. I tried everything I could think of: checked the plug switch, tried a socket that I knew to be working, trying different cables. With battery running out, I did the last resort: close browser, and turn it off and on again. A few seconds later:
“Ding!”
Aaaand the battery’s mysteriously charging again.
Other things have gone wrong in the past when I’ve delayed updates. It feels as if my computer has a sentience, and is so desperate to update [via restarting] that various parts of it start going on strike until its demands are met.
Possibly by design, possibly not. Maybe I’m just seeing patterns that are not there.
Still. Why, why, a thousand “why”s that will never be answered in a way that yields any understanding or future mitigation.
Why couldn’t I restore my tabs like I normally do?
Why do things mysteriously start to go wrong in a ways that encourage me to restart?
Why does so much of the content of the “essential” updates consist of stuff I never ever use nor plan to use?
Why does updating software require a restart?
Why should updating software require a restart?
Heck, why does restarting kill open applications by default?
Why don’t things just stay around until you decide to finish with them?
Why is the filesystem preserved between restarts but the contents of RAM not automatically persisted?
As in – why is the default mode of computer interaction a dynamic desktop that must be cleared at the end of each day, and a static filing cabinet that you have to load+unload from?
Why do we put up with this?
Why do I feel powerless to do anything significant about it within a timeframe of less than an entire lifetime of work?
Also, while I’m at it,
Why do I have to figure out how to safely “root” my phone to be able to delete the litany of huge preinstalled Google apps I never use nor intend to use, in order for me to upgrade Android, and to free enough space to download $(turns out I suddenly need this random app that is the only way to pay for / use real-world service X that I happen to unexpectedly need)
And why does Bluetooth File Transfer say “file not sent” without any other information that could help me perturb variables of my situation to try and get the damn photo to transfer?
We like to think, and sometimes lament, that as programmers, we are an elite few having access to the full potential of computer software while the average shlub is forced to just accept what they’re given.
When the scope is making little apps and websites, there’s a lot of truth to this. But as far as the common-case, everyday experience of using computers goes — because even the 1337est haxxors at your workplace use a screen and a GUI to manage their terminals — how much effective control do we actually exercise over the universal simulators that we bought and consider our “own” posessions?
attachments.fallback: Programming, Made Complicated Link: The everyday reality

Hey, <@U5STGTB3J> and <@UCQ4BBFQ9> - great blog post and reflections. Two related things come to mind, about the two themes which have come up, which could be described as "ownability" and "durability". Firstly, Philip Tchernavskij and my PPIG 2018 paper in which we argue that most technological artefacts are not even theoretically ownable - <https://github.com/amb26/papers/blob/master/ppig-2018/What_Lies_in_the_Path_of_the_Revolution.pdf> . Secondly, a recent tweet of <@U6KQ2S410>’s about an interesting new system, Urbit, written by a politically unsavory individual - <https://twitter.com/jonathoda/status/918157356784955393> - "a formal model of the system does not contain an operation which unpredictably erases half its brain"

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/jonathoda|@jonathoda>: "a formal model of the system does not contain an operation which unpredictably erases half its brain" <http://media.urbit.org/whitepaper.pdf>

text: Yes, Fauconnier's "mental spaces" indeed tantalisingly suggest that they could be operationalised. The only work I've seen in this area so far is Oli Sharpe's semiotic programming language "Semprola" <http://semprola.blogspot.com/> - which I've urged him to extend in a more Lakoffian direction.

attachments.title: Semprola - A Semiotic Programming Language
attachments.text: Semiotic Programming (SP) is an approach to programming computers in the age of ubiquitous, networked computing.

text: <@U8A5MS6R1> - indeed, Lakoff himself has already argued that mathematics is an inescapably metaphorical discipline. His "Where Mathematics Comes From" was one of <@UDCLA1HU4>’s early suggestions for our Revolutionary Reading Group - <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Where_Mathematics_Comes_From> . When you say "we almost always misunderstand stuff", you are referring to the process of understanding itself - that is, what you refer to as misunderstanding is actually understanding :slightly_smiling_face:

attachments.title: Where Mathematics Comes From
attachments.text: Where Mathematics Comes From: How the Embodied Mind Brings Mathematics into Being (hereinafter WMCF) is a book by George Lakoff, a cognitive linguist, and Rafael E. Núñez, a psychologist. Published in 2000, WMCF seeks to found a cognitive science of mathematics, a theory of embodied mathematics based on conceptual metaphor.

REBL - Stuart Halloway <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c52QhiXsmyI>
REBL - Stuart Halloway <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c52QhiXsmyI>
REBL is a graphical, interactive tool for browsing Clojure data.
REBL is a graphical, interactive tool for browsing Clojure data.

a two-pane browser/viewer system for viewing collections and their contents
    navigation into and back out of nested collections
    a structured editor pane for entering expressions to be evaluated
    a root browse of a history of expression evaluations
    the ability to 'wrap' a stdio based REPL (e.g. Clojure's native REPL) so that it can monitor and display the interactions therein, while still allowing them to flow back to the host (e.g. the editor)
    when used with non-stdio repls (e.g. nREPL), can be launched a la carte and accepts values to inspect via an API call
    the ability to capture nested values as defs in the user namespace
    the ability to run multiple independent UI windows
    metadata viewing
    datafy support
    extensibility to new browsers and viewers
    full keyboard control via hotkeys
